After machine joined in Azure AD and then restarted, the machine may get stuck during boot for about 3 hours (2.7 hours). After this duration it finally gets unstuck and the boot sequence completes. Complete dumps taken from system hung during boot show a dead lock in Services.exe and LSA initialization thread in Lsass.exe.
rootCause: This issue occurs in the following condition: The impacted computer is Azure AD joined, and the workgroup name is the same as the on-premises domain’s NetBIOS name. You have logged on to this computer with a federated user at least once. The ~3 hour hang occurs because in this condition, Windows unexpectedly evaluates a condition in which a wait time of 10,000 seconds is initiated. This timeout condition is manifested as a three-hour startup delay. (10,000 seconds = 167 minutes = 2 hours 47 minutes). LsapWaitForSamForAwhile() code.
solution: To resolve this issue, use a workgroup name that does not match the NetBIOS name of the on-premises domain

Full title not trunaced by our wonderful tools: AAD: Sync: Objects including group members from trusted domains fail to sync if FSP container not in sync scope Problem Statement An an on-prem Group syncronized to Azure Active Directory / Office 365 contains members from a trusted domain. The trusted users do not appears as members of the same group in O365 User accounts in the local doamin are correctedly synced to Azure AD but users from the trusted domain are not visible in O365 Specific example The saxobank. ch domain is part of one forest. The saxobank. com domain is part of another forest. There is a bi-directional trust between the two forests and the users are synced to tenant saxobank.onmicrosoft.com via one AADConnect server, from the saxobank. com domain. The users from the saxobank. ch domain which are part of a Group in the saxobank. com domain, are not synced into the cloud as Members of that group. But the users themselves are synched successfully into o365. Example: Group name: APPACC_LVE_WorkDay_Users (Group Scope � Domain local) Affected user: alventosa@saxobank. ch - not synced as a group member Working user: lba@saxobank. com - synced During our remote session we have created a new test group which is part of the same OU with the one listed above. Group name: APPACC_tst_lbatest (Group Scope � Domain local) Affected user: alventosa@saxobank. ch - not synced as a group member Working user: lba@saxobank. com - synced
rootCause: Short Answer: The ForeignSecurityPrincipals container was not part of the sync scope in AADConnect. Longer answer and problem backgrounder For the case when a Group in Forest1 has members in the current Forest1 and from a different Forest, Forest2I would expect to see : #1 in the AD Connector for Forest 1 the Group object (eg. G1 in Forest1) member references to Local Domain Users in this Forest that are members of this Group member references to ForeignSecurityPrincipal objects in this Forest. These FSPs point to the actual User objects in Forest2 the User objects in Forest1 ForeignSecurityPrincipal objects in this Forest1 (which point to User objects in Forest2) #2 in the AD Connector for Forest2 the User objects (which are pointed to by the FSPs in Forest1) When the Group F1:G1 should synchronized from AD Connector for Forest1 to the MV: - All member references will flow IFF there is a matching linked User object in the MV. So for all the member references to flow to MV, you need: - the User objects from Forest1 to flow to MV. - The User objects from Forest2 to flow to MV - The FSPs from Forest1 to link to the User object from Forest2 in the MV So the first thing I would check is what is in the MV for that particular Group which is having a problem, what do the member references look like?
solution: Add the FSP container to the sync scope in AADConnect Perform a full sync

symptom: Consider the following scenario: You have an ADFS 3.0 farm using a dedicated SQL instance to store the configuration data. You try to add an additional server to the existing farm using the Server Manager configuration wizard. On the Pre-requisite Checks page, the following error occurs: �One or more prerequisites failed. Please fix these issues and click �Rerun prerequisite check� The type initializer for 'System.Data.SqlClient.SqlConnection' threw an exception. Unable to retrieve configuration from the SQL database. The type initializer for 'System.Data.SqlClient.SqlConnection' threw an exception. Note: This same error occurs when using PowerShell command Add-AdfsFarmNode
rootCause: Not entirely known, but it seems to be a permission issue on the local machine for the ADFS service account. Possibly a problem in the local security policies or lack of permission to something on the local file system. Unfortunately, we were not able to run a Procmon trace the time, but we didn�t see anything wrong in the Security event log. Note: The error makes you think it�s a problem communicating with the SQL server, but in a Netmon trace, we saw no communication whatsoever with the SQL box. The error occurs when trying to create the SQL connection object on the ADFS server.
solution: Add the ADFS service account to the local Administrators group on the ADFS box.

Windows 7 Client machine in Forest A calls DClocator to discover a Domain Controller in Forest B Forest A and Forest B User and Client machine belongs to Forest A. Application is hosted on Forest B Users are accessing Application in Forest B from a client machine domain-joined in Forest A When users connect to office network via VPN, corresponding AD site is "PR" When user access Application hosted in Forest B, Network trace shows a Site-specific DNS query to locate a Domain controller in Forest B. The Trace shows client adds its own site name in the request but the site name referred in the request does not exist / is not defined in Forest B DNS communication Win 7 ---- > _ldap._tcp._sites.dc._msdcs.ForestBDomainName to Client primary DNS server but returned with no such name and returned the conditional forwarder DNS server name in response No Such name SRV _ldap._tcp._sites.dc._msdcs.ForestBDomainName SOA <Conditional forwarder Name in Forest B> Netlogon showing below error [CRITICAL] NetpDcGetDcNext: _ldap._tcp._sites.dc._msdcs.ForestBDomainName .: Cannot Query DNS. 9002 0x232a [CRITICAL] NetpDcGetNameIp: ForestBDomainName: No data returned from DnsQuery. [MISC] NetpDcGetName: NetpDcGetNameIp returned 1355
rootCause: Ideally, Win 7 should trigger a generic query with Forest B domain name but is not doing it Win 8 and onwards if Site specific query fails, fallback to Domain specific query, in this case observed Win8 machine connected on VPN send _ldap._tcp.dc._msdcs.ForestBDomainName and gets the response from DNS server.
solution: Workaround: Create an Empty site corresponding the "client domain site" in Forest B AD sites and services Next time Win7 send site specific query, it is being answered and DC locator discover the Domain controller in Forest B

rootCause: SQL Permissions missing on the SQL Express database.
solution: Shut down SQL Express Server from services. Open cmd window (as admin) and run single-user mode as local admin with this command: c:\Program Files\Microsoft SQL Server\MSSQL10.SQLEXPRESS\MSSQL\Binn\sqlservr.exe" -m -s SQLEXPRESS Open another cmd window (as admin) and access this location. c:\Program Files\Microsoft SQL Server\MSSQL10.SQLEXPRESS\MSSQL\Binn\ Open sqlcmd: sqlcmd -S .\SQLEXPRESS Now add the sysadmin user: sp_addsrvrolemember 'domain\user', 'sysadmin' GO # Now Ctrl+C the single-user mode from the first cmd window to kill SQL Server. # Restart the SQL Express service from Services.msc in a normal way.

symptom: Title: AAD: UserCertificate attribute value enforcement rollout in March 2017 Microsoft will begin rolling out a stricter enforcement of the number of attribute values accepted by multi-valued binary properties (such as UserCertificate & UserSMIMECertificate) in March 2017 The new limit will be 15 values accepted for each multi-valued binary property (currently, this applies only to UserCertificate & UserSMIMECertificate). Exceeding the limit on a new user will result in a provisioning failure of that one user as opposed to the Exchange behavior which is to filter out the excess values. The optimal solution would be to resolve the attribute into certs, filter out all expired certs, and only flow active certs. That should really be fewer than 10. Resolving certs can be a perf impact so should only be done when there are many certs If an existing customer already has more than 15 values in place for an existing object, Microsoft will not block attempts to modify that user�s other properties. Microsoft will only prevent the creation of new objects with more than 15 values for those attributes Existing objects from increasing above 15 values. If an existing object has more than 15 values, Microsoft will not block attempts to decrease the number of values, even if the final number is above 15. (i.e. syncing a change on an existing object with 30 values to have 29 values will be allowed). A LargeObject failure today is equivalent to a bad request. It doesn�t show up as a failure, because it just means the client is trying to do something that is not allowed. This behavior change expands the definition of disallowed operation to better protect our service for all tenants. Microsoft is planning to implement another type of filter where we sum up the total byte size of the object across all multi-valued attributes to protect the service more broadly across all attributes The POR for rolling out this enforcement is shown in the table below: MT = megatenant partition Date In scope for enforcement Monday: 3/6 MSIT + 10 active tenants (detailed below) Tuesday: 3/7 Partition 38 3/8 Partition 37 + 36 (MT) + 35 (MT) + 34 (MT) 3/9 33+32+31+30(MT)+29(MT) 3/10 28+27+24+23+22(MT)+21 Monday: 3/13 19+18+17+15+14 3/14 16+13+12+11+10 3/15 9+8+7+6+5 3/16 4+3+2+1
solution: AAD Connect Syncs all objects Limit was previously not enforced in MSODS. Now it is. Errors surface in client. MSODS returns a "large object" error which shows up in old sync UI. Tenants get sync email notification. Eventually this and other errors will get exposed in the sync errors UI in the portals. Contact Phillip LeBlanc, Chun Yong Chua, Brad Craig; Bruce

symptom: Unable to configure ADFS 2016 Role on Windows Server 2016. End Goal:- First add ADFS Server 2016 to the existing farm of ADFS Server 2012 R2 and once it is done successfully, make it as primary Federation Server. ADFS Role is installed on the Domain Controller. There are 2 ADFS Servers 2016:- DCOLY02 and DCOLY01 This is the first time setup.
rootCause: All 3 ADFS Certificates were not present in Personal Computer Store of DCOLY02 . We have a scenario where for ADFS 3.0 all the three Certificates i.e ADFS Service Communication Certificate, Token- Signing and Token- Decrypting Certificate were all issued by Public CA.... In these scenario, if we are Configuring ADFS Server Role, all 3 Certificates shall be imported in Personal Computer Store of the respective ADFS Server which was not there in this scenario. Error Message observed was"- certificate with the thumbprint xxx2F5DA7321EC5CB78EB0FA6C332B58D65xxxxx was not found in the LocalMachine certificate store.
solution: We would need to export all the 3 Certificates from HBEADFS05 to DCOLY02 .Unfortunately we werent able to copy the ADFS Token-Decrypting Cert along with Private Key from HBEADFS05. In case , we are not able to export private key of the Token Signing or Token- Decryption Certificate, we need to create Self Signed certificate. Note:- If Token Signing and Token Decryption Certificates are SELF Signed, no need to copy them to Personal Computer Store of the respective ADFS Server where we are configuring ADFS role. If Token Signing and Token Decryption Certificates are issued by Public CA, we need to copy them along with Private Key to the Personal Computer Store of the respective ADFS Server where we are configuring ADFS role. Now,. if you have to generate Self Signed Certificate for Token- Decrypting Cert , we need to first create Certificate Sharing container in AD using following then create Self Signed Cert.... a) Set-AdfsCertSharingContainer -ServiceAccount <Service Account under which ADFS Service is running> Then create Self signed Cert b) Set-AdfsProperties -AutoCertificateRollover $True------> enable Auto Cert Rollver. c) Update-AdfsCertificate -CertificateType Token-Decrypting-----> Will create Token-Decrypting Cert. d) Update-AdfsCertificate -CertificateType Token-Signing---> Will create Token-signing Cert. e) Set-AdfsProperties -AutoCertificateRollover $False ----> Set auto rollover to false. Move the Primary Role to ADFS2016 1. Open Powershell as Administrator and type the following command to verify the ADFS server role: Get-AdfsSyncProperties 2. Move the Primary ADFS Server Role to ADFS2016 with the following command: Set-AdfsSyncProperties -Role PrimaryComputer Run the following command to set server ADFS2012R2 as secondary server Invoke-Command -ComputerName <FQDN Of Primary ADFS 2012 R2 Computer name> { Set-AdfsSyncProperties -Role SecondaryComputer -PrimaryComputerName <FQDN of ADFS 2016 Server which you want to configure as Primary> c. Invoke-Command -ComputerName hbeadfs05.corp.wahbexchange.org { Set-AdfsSyncProperties -Role SecondaryComputer -PrimaryComputerName dcoly02.corp.wahbexchange.org f) From GUi, make the self Signed Certs as primary.

symptom: The command used in : Method 1) step2 a) must be changed to � connect-AzureAD� instead of � connect-MSOLService� all command running graph V2 and the first step used to run AAD PS V1 Adding ========== the V1 as method 2 it was before and replace with V2 connect-MSOLService Note When you're prompted, enter non-federated cloud service global administrator credentials. 2.Set-MsolUserPrincipalName -UserPrincipalName [ExistingUPN] -NewUserPrincipalName [DefaultDomainUPN] Note In this command, [ExistingUPN] represents the current UPN of the user ID, and [DefaultDomainUPN] represents the UPN of the user ID that has the domain suffix changed to the default domain. This is a partner article to 3216953 AAD: Auth: Account Lockout mitigation for user accounts exposed on the extranet Office 365 tenants will soon be able to self-manage domain-wide IP Block Lists via PowerShell on: Standard IPv4 and IPv6 addresses IP ranges Customers with on-prem ADFS Servers that federate with Azure Active Directory or Office 365 may use this self-service block list to prevent basic auth request from tenant-specified IP addresses directed to EXO from being proxied to the tenants federation endpoint. The block list applies to successful and unsuccessful down-level authentication requests regardless of whether AAD or Office 365 services are consumed by the tenant or the account referenced in the logon requests. IP Block ranges may be submitted using the following formats: CIDR format 2001:0DB8::CD3/60 High-Low format 192.168.0.1-192.168.0.254 sub masking format 192.168.8.2(255.255.255.0) This article describes how a tenant could implement the block list and confirm the results of a self-service block list submission Note: Federated tenants ADFS Servers may receive modern authentication requests directly from the caller or proxied via EXO Windows Server2012 R2 and alter ADFS Servers log event 412 for successful authentication requests and Event 411 for unsuccessful authentication requests 411 + 412 events that contain a single IP address identify the IP address of the originating callers IP that negotiated using modern auth 411 + 412 events that contain two IP addresses identify the IP address of the originating caller followed by the EXO IP addresse that proxied a basic auth request to the tenants federation endpoint (ADFS server or 3rd party equivalent) This article is solely focused o the blocking of IPs proxied by EXO using basic auth Tenants wishing to bloc modern auth request should do so at their on-prem firewall.

SourceAnchor and ImmutableID hard match changes in March + May 2017. Customers use Hard-Match (SourceAnchor) to link objects between AD and AAD. Azure AD Sync Service uses SourceAnchor as pivot and won't allow global admin to update it while object is synchronized. Tenants open cases to update SourceAnchor due to design change or recovery from duplicate objects.
rootCause: As of March 2017, Azure AD team updated backend service code to prevent using scoping filter method to modify SourceAnchor attribute. After Premier customer escalation, PG extended deadline to May 15th 2017.
solution: 1) Don't use ObjectGUID as SourceAnchor for multi-forest - pick SourceAnchor not dependent on AD forest. 2) To change SourceAnchor for single/few objects: a) Deactivate DirSync on tenant (impact up to 72 hours), OR b) Run Set-MSOLUser -ImmutableID to update value.

Application integrated with Azure AD fails to get Access Token for SharePoint. Error AADSTS65005: The client application has requested access to resource. This request has failed because the client has not specified this resource in its requiredResourceAccess list. Also: AADSTS65005: Invalid resource - resource not listed in requested permissions in client application registration.
rootCause: Misconfiguration of Application ID URI in AppgraphInterchange application conflicting with SharePoint resource URL, affecting access of another application (Test ADAL Auth) via code.
solution: Changed Application ID URI from conflicting https://sohodragon.sharepoint.com to https://sohodragon.sharepoint.com/test in AppgraphInterchange app. Changed target URL in Test ADAL Auth from sohodragon-my.sharepoint.com to sohodragon.sharepoint.com. Both applications working after fix.

symptom: After in-place upgrading from DirSync to AAD Connect build 1.1.443.0, objects no longer synced from AD to AAD/O365. AAD Connector has no run profiles when there should be ~5. Event ID 905: Run profile Full Import not found. Scheduler stuck performing full import every 30 minutes.
rootCause: Build 1.1.443.0 (March 6 2017) contained a regression in the DirSync upgrade path.
solution: 1) Use CreateAADRunProfiles.ps1 script to repair AAD Connector run profiles (immediate recovery). 2) If script does not solve, perform parallel mode using preserved DirSync settings (AzureADConnect.exe /migrate). Settings found in %programData%/AADConnect. 3) Problem resolved in AAD Connect versions after 1.1.443.0.

organization has deployed AD RMS and wants to migrate to Azure Information
Protection, the normal migration process involves exporting the root keys from the
AD RMS cluster and importing them into Azure RMS, a service that is part of the
Azure Information Protection platform. 

 

In some
cases, an AD RMS cluster may have been configured in a way that prevents the
key from being exported and imported to the cloud. This may happen if the keys
have been marked as non-exportable, were protected with an HSM that is not
directly supported by Azure Information Protection or if the key is in a
Hardware Security Module for which the operator cards or other artifacts required
for a key export are not available.
solution:
A Fix
for the MSIPC RMS client used by Office has been released that allows these
clients to continue using AD RMS to consume previously protected content
without granting the ability to protect new content with these keys, while
allowing the clients to protect and consume content with Azure RMS. 

This
update is available from the Microsoft Update Web site:

http://update.microsoft.com

This
fix is available for Office 2013 and Office 2016, as well as other MSIPC
applications developed with the MSIPC SDK 2.1.

The following
versions of Office include this capability:

Office 2013          >= 15.0.4849.1000

Office 2016 MSI  >= 16.0.4496.1000 

Office 2016 C2R  >= 16.0.7407.1000

During a typical migration from AD RMS to Azure Information Protection, the root key of the AD RMS cluster used to protect content (called the Server Licensor Certificate, or SLC) is exported from the cluster and imported into the Azure RMS service part of the Azure Information Protection platform. Windows clients are then configured to redirect requests for licenses to open content protected with the AD RMS cluster to the Azure RMS service, which then issues the licenses as per the policy in the document and other artifacts that allow the client to use AD RMS for protecting content. Among these artifacts is the Client Licensor Certificate, or CLC for short, obtained by the client by calling APIs available under Publish.asmx in the AD RMS cluster. Once issued this certificate allows a client to protect content with keys associated to the AD RMS cluster�s SLC key.By denying access to these APIs, a client that has this fix installed that is configured to use Azure RMS can consume content from AD RMS without receiving a CLC by AD RMS. This allows the client to continue using Azure RMS to protect all new content while retaining access to content previously protected with AD RMS, even if the AD RMS key has not been imported into Azure RMS. This allows for a gradual phase-out of the AD RMS infrastructure as new content gets protected using Azure RMS, until the content protected with AD RMS has been re-protected with the new keys in Azure RMS, or it is no longer relevant, at which point the AD RMS cluster and its SLC can be decommissioned. After installing the fix on Windows clients running Office 2013 or Office 2016, and/or other applications developed using the MSIPC SDK, to set AD RMS in read-only mode you need to deny access to the Publish.asmx page. To do this, use the following steps:1)     Log on to each AD RMS server that is used by users to protect content, click Start, point to Administrative Tools, and then click Internet Information Services (IIS) Manager.2)     If the User Account Control dialog box appears, confirm that the action it displays is what you want, and then click Continue. 3)     Expand the domain node, expand Sites, expand Default Web Site, and then expand _wmcs.4)     Note that your installation of AD RMS may have been done in a web site other than the Default Web Site, in which case you will need to adjust the path above accordingly.5)     Right-click the licensing folder, and then click Switch to Content View.6)     Right-click Publish.asmx, and then click Switch to Features View.7)     Under IIS, double-click Authentication, check that Anonymous Authentication is disabled.8)     Right-click the licensing directory again, and then click Switch to Content View.9)     Right-click Publish.asmx, and then click Edit Permissions...10)  In the Security pane, click on Edit and click Add�11)  Enter the name of the group you want to prevent from protecting content using AD RMS. To deny all users the ability to protect content with AD RMS, enter Everyone.12)  Click OK13)  Close IIS ManagerOnce IIS is configured this way on all the AD RMS nodes, you can confirm a client can use AD RMS in Read-Only mode by starting with a client that is not configured to use either AD RMS or Azure RMS and opening content that was protected using AD RMS. After that, try to protect new content. You will not be able to perform this operation.A client that has been configured to use Azure RMS will also be able to consume content from AD RMS without affecting its prior configuration with Azure RMS. After consuming content from AD RMS such a client will continue to protect content using Azure RMS. If done as part of the de-provisioning process for AD RMS, you should also use the AD RMS console to unpublish the AD RMS Service Connection Point.

rootCause:
This
issue is being triggered by the PTA specific call to Msol-GetUser on the main
logon path of AAD Connect where multiple
separate commands issued on the same pipeline are being accidentally chained
together when PowerShell transcription is enabledEach
PowerShellProvider has its own local copy of a powerShell pipeline
object.  However it appears PowerShell itself has a common object for
transcriptionThe use of nested clauses also causes problems when transcription is enabled.
solution:
Install corrective updatesInstall Windows Management Framework 5.0 from https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/download/confirmation.aspx?id=50395 to enable the transcription functionality. Install the May 2017 or later release of AAD Connect which allows the basic express/custom flow for non-ADFS scenarios to work when transcription is enabledRelease notes for the May 2017 release of AAD Connect describe this fix as follows:

Previously, you must disable PowerShell transcription for Azure
AD Connect wizard to run correctly. This issue is partially resolved. You can
enable PowerShell transcription if you are using Azure AD Connect wizard to
manage sync configuration. You must disable PowerShell transcription if you are
using Azure AD Connect wizard to manage ADFS configuration.Install the June 2017 release of AAD Connect which includes the ADFS partition of the fix Workaround and action plan for versions of AAD Connect that precede 82346: ICM: Enabling the PS transcription policy makes the AAD Connect wizard grumpy (input object cannot be found).  PowerShell transcription is not supported with these versions of AAD Connect.The AAD Connect Wizard will not run correctly with PowerShell pipeline transactions enabled, since they fundamentally change how PowerShell works (as far as the AAD Connect Wizard is concerned ). Misc.reg file to enable PowerShell transcription.  This will enable PS transcription for the currently logged on user.  The transcription folder is called PSTranscript and will be located in My Documents.Copy text between "start here" and "end here" Save as PowerShellTranscription.regWindows Registry Editor Version 5.00[HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell\Transcription]"EnableTranscripting"=dword:00000001"OutputDirectory"="PsTranscript""EnableInvocationHeader"=dword:00000001

symptom:
There may be circumstances where the replica volume for a
protected data source is under allocated or a very large increase of protected
data results in synchronization or recovery point job failures due to
inadequate space on the replica.

Some common examples of where this might occur are explained
below.

Bare Metal
Restore (BMR) protection.    In
DPM, BMR protection covers protection for operating system files (System State)
and critical volumes (excluding user data). DPM does not calculate the
size of BMR data source, but assumes 20 GB for all servers. At the time of
protection this cannot be changed in the disk allocation page when using Modern
Backup Storage (MBS).

symptom:
After installing KB4015547 on Windows Server 2012 R2 DC, you may notice Kerberos Key Distribution Center (KDC) Service fails to start.   net start kdc command returns error 87: the parameter is incorrect 
 
  
  Screenshot 
  
  
   Textual copy of error 
  
 
 
  
   
  
  
  net start kdc The Kerberos key distribution center service is starting The Kerberos key distribution center service could not be startedA system error has occurred.System error 87 has occurred. The parameter is incorrect.  
  
  You may also notice the failure from system event log, an error like below will be logged.  Log Name:      System Source:        Service Control Manager Event ID:      7023 Task Category: None Level:         Error Keywords:      Classic Description: The Kerberos Key Distribution Center service terminated with the following error: The parameter is incorrect.
rootCause: This is a packaging issue, Because KB4015547 (April 11th 2017 security only update) only updates kdcsvc.dll, when a machine which has an old version of lsasrv.dll (older than 6.3.9600.18581, from March 2017 update), there will be a mismatch between the new kdcsvc.dll and old lsasrv.dll, a enumerate data structure used in both dlls has different element ranges, This causes KDC service failed to start.
solution: Workarounds:If you can consider installing monthly update, not security only update, you can install KB4015550 (April 2017 monthly update)  instead of KB4015547 (April 2017 security only update) . KB4015550 is April 2017 monthly update, it updates both lsasrv.dll to 6.3.9600.18581 and kdcsvc.dll to 6.3.9600.18652, which can fix the mismatch. OR If you want to only install security only update, you need to install both KB4015547 (April 2017 security only update) and KB4012213 (March 2017 Security only update). KB4012213 updates lsasrv.dll to v6.3.9600.18581

symptom:
Symptom from 117031715473638We have issues with Outlook clients, across all users, maintaining auth to Office 365. It's behaving as though the Refresh Tokens are getting revoked intermittently; sometimes it's weeks, sometimes it's days, and there have been reports of multiple times per day, and affects at least 6 Office 365 tenants federated through our Okta tenant. The issue didn't start out having a high impact to our business. But after an entire month of back-and-forth with Office 365 support on the issue, having received no help with the issue the impact to our business has increased significantly and is becoming extremely close to becoming damaging as we are about to add $2m/year in business for us to our Okta tenant and currently the user experience is already making people unhappy with the token revocations.The Refresh Token Max Inactive Time policy was set to  hour String: MaxInactiveTime Affects: Refresh tokens Summary: This policy controls how old a refresh token can be before a client can no longer use it to retrieve a new access/refresh token pair when attempting to access this resource. Because a new refresh token usually is returned when a refresh token is used, this policy prevents access if the client tries to access any resource by using the current refresh token during the specified period of time. At exactly one hour the refresh token is invalid and the user is force to reauthenticate.  This occurs even though the user is active in Outlook 2016 or other mail clientsFiddler traces show the following signature{"error":"invalid_grant","error_description":"AADSTS70002: Error validating credentials. AADSTS70008: The refresh token has expired due to inactivity. The token was issued on 2017-03-11T04:27:59.5264175Z and was inactive for 14.00:00:00.
Trace ID: 9d871ae9-7040-4dd7-ada5-5bd3ffb91100
Correlation ID: 6471cd7c-d45b-456b-8c91-34f6fd09afbd
Timestamp: 2017-03-25 04:50:19Z","error_codes":[70002,70008],"timestamp":"2017-03-25 04:50:19Z","trace_id":"9d871ae9-7040-4dd7-ada5-5bd3ffb91100","correlation_id":"6471cd7c-d45b-456b-8c91-34f6fd09afbd"} In a 2nd test tenant also using Octa as the IDP Access token lifetime set to 1 hourMaxInactiveTime set to 1 hour instead of 2 hours The client connected to that test tenant behaved exactly as one would normally expect it to. It did not arbitrarily disconnect every hour. The client remained persistently authed for several hours before reverting the settings back to default and ending the test. This tenant is apparently not affected by the same issue as our production tenant, but it is a similar environment; users are synced from our AD, using Okta as both the sync agent and our Idp, auth delegated to AD, with a federated domain. A near carbon-copy, minus the users and other tenant-specific data."Backgrounder on refresh token lifetime User authenticates to Azure-STS using ADALIf AzureSTS receives password change notifications, which can be sync�d form on premise. ADAL gets a Refresh token which is good for 2 weeks, it will be renewed if used before the two weeks are up until the 90 days are hit. Token Expiry Time = Min (Token Last Use Time + 14 days, Auth code redemption time + 90 days) If ADAL uses the refresh token within 2 weeks, SSP will get a new refresh token for 90 days every time it goes for a refreshed access token If ADAL does not use the refresh token within 2 weeks of token reissuance, User will be prompted to login to Azure-STS again If password for the User changes any time, IWs will be prompted to login only when the access token has burnt out (after its lifetime of an hour) If AuzreSTS does not receive password change notifications ADAL gets a Refresh token for 12 hours and access token for 1 hour, refresh token will be renewed every time the access token is refreshed within 12 hours. ser are prompted to login after every 12 hours of inactivity at the ADAL.
rootCause:
This behavior is by-design due to the LastPasswordChangeTimestamp not being synchronized for the user profiles. The issue stems from the fact that the code path diverged.  Tenants created prior to August 2016 follow this process for user profiles that do not synchronize the LastPasswordChangeTimestamp with regards to the Refresh Token. Consider the following workflow: User Authenticates User is issued a Refresh Token The tenant has the Refresh Token Max Inactive Time set to 2 hours The end user begins to use the Outlook 2016 thick client At two hours, the user is forced to authenticate and we notice that the Refresh Token is not "sliding" A decision was made to not issue new Refresh Tokens to users whose current password cannot be verified.  This is to prevent the reuse of "old" Refresh Token for users whose passwords might have expiredTenants that were created after August 2016 follow the following process for user profiles that do not synchronize the LastPasswordChangeTimestamp with regards to the Refresh Token. User Authenticates User is issued a Refresh Token The tenant has the Refresh Token Max Inactive Time set to 2 hours The end user begins to use the Outlook 2016 thick client We continue to issue new Refresh tokens for these users who are constantly active in the client.  The absolute lifetime for the Refresh Token is 12 hours, regardless of the value that you have configured for Refresh Token Max Inactive TimeLong versionUser authenticates to Azure-STS using ADAL If AzureSTS receives password change notifications, which
can be sync�d form on premise. ADAL gets a Refresh token which is good for 2
weeks, it will be renewed if used before the two weeks are up until the 90 days
are hit. Token Expiry Time = Min (Token Last Use Time + 14 days, Auth
code redemption time + 90 days) 

 If ADAL uses the refresh token within 2 weeks, SSP will
get a new refresh token for 90 days every time it goes for a refreshed access
token 

 If ADAL does not use the refresh token within 2 weeks of
token reissuance, User will be prompted to login to Azure-STS again 

 If password for the User changes any time, IWs will be
prompted to login only when the access token has burnt out (after its lifetime
of an hour) 

 If AuzreSTS does not receive password change
notifications 

 ADAL gets a Refresh token for 12 hours and access token
for 1 hour, refresh token will be renewed every time the access token is
refreshed within 12 hours. 

 User are prompted to login after every 12 hours of
inactivity at the ADAL.
solution: Change the Okta profile synchronization configuration to include the LastPasswordChangeTimestamp

symptom: After machine joined in Azure AD and then restart, machine hangs at boot up. After waiting for about 3 hours it finally boots up.
rootCause: The machine was in a workgroup before joining to Azure Domain, the workgroup name is either the same name with the Azure AD domain name, or there is a NetBIOS Alias using the same name on the AD domain.
solution: Move the machine back to workgroup, and then change the machine workgroup name to the default name "WORKGROUP".  then rejoin to Azure AD.

The Cross-Tenant solution for China provides O365 customers who require separate tenants a set of collaboration scenarios between their two tenants. The solution requires one tenant to be within Office 365 China and another within Office 365 worldwide. Microsoft assists in the configuration of specific tenants on an as need basis to help customers who have clear requirements for separate tenants and does not publish configuration documentation publicly. The following article describes how you can troubleshoot issues with Azure AD components in the Azure portal @ https://portal.azure.com (Ibiza). For Introductory information on Azure AD in Ibiza please review the following Learning Central Training... https://learn.microsoft.com/course/81666/launch
When clicking on Open with Explorer in a SharePoint document library, the error 'Access Denied' is received when authentication is handled by the Azure Application Proxy
rootCause: When Azure Application Proxy is used for authentication, the cookie created for authentication is a session cookie.
solution: Since Open in Explorer view (which relies on the WebClient service) requires persistent cookies, it will not function unless Azure App Proxy is set up for pass-through authentication.

symptom: https://social.technet.microsoft.com/wiki/contents/articles/19901.dirsync-list-of-attributes-that-are-synced-by-the-azure-active-directory-sync-tool.aspx describes the list of attributes synchronized by an AAD identify sync clients but is silent as to whether Account lockouts are synchronized from on-prem AD to O365 \ Azure Active DirectoryThe disabling of a user account in on-prem AD is synchronized to O365 \ AAD Existing documentation for attributes synchronized by AAD Connectindicate that the identity sync clients update the AccountEnabled attribute in Azure AD This content is less clear that the account disabled state is derived from UAC AccountDisabled flag.
Known issue where if customers try to use ADFS Authentication through an ILB/VPN, they are unable to. Topology:Local Client -> VPN -> ILB -> ADFS server(s)
rootCause: The issue is due to a problem with how ILBs handle the jumbo packets sent by ADFS servers as SSL "Server Hello. Certificates". Once they reach the Azure Gateway, they are not fragmented properly so they do not make it across the VPN tunnel.
solution: Solution is to reduce the MTU packet size on the ADFS servers by executing:netsh interface ipv4 show subinterfacesnetsh interface ipv4 set subinterface "Sub-interface name from previous command" mtu=1350 store=persistentYou can point customers to this solution on the Azure Diagnostics blade:

symptom: Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to inform customers that a new version of Azure Active Directory (AD) Connect is available that addresses an Important security vulnerability. The update addresses a vulnerability that could allow elevation of privilege if Azure AD Connect Password writeback is misconfigured during enablement. An attacker who successfully exploited this vulnerability could reset passwords and gain unauthorized access to arbitrary on-premises AD privileged user accounts. The issue is addressed in the latest version (1.1.553.0) of Azure AD Connect by not allowing arbitrary password reset to on-premises AD privileged user accounts.
rootCause: The issue is addressed in the latest version (1.1.553.0) of Azure AD Connect by not allowing arbitrary password reset to on-premises AD privileged user accounts.
solution: See https://technet.microsoft.com/library/security/4033453.aspxInstall the 1.1.553.0 version of AAD Connect

symptom: Abstract The AAD Connect client supports auto-upgrading from older to newer versions of the identity sync client. The AAD Connect product group is continually increasing the scope of AAD Connect configurations that eligible for auto-update. On or around July 21st, version 1.1.561 of AAD Connect was released for Auto-Upgrade which exposed a DCOM API dependency which causes an access violation in the auto-update process which subsequently fails to complete. That auto-update process has since been halted. Version 1.1.561 has now been removed from AutoUpgrade and a new version of AAD Connect will be pushed out to fix broken systems. Customers that open support calls for sync failures caused by the auto-update process should confirm a scenario match using signatures in the symptoms section of this article which can be corrected using the steps in the resolution section. Impacted tenants can be broken down into 1 of 3 scenarios:Scheduler is suspended. No object synchronization is occurring Scheduler is suspended. Customer also has PHS and PHS is not syncing Scheduler is not suspended. Object synchronization is fine. But PHS is disabled Symptoms Customers may call Microsoft Support in and after July 2017 stating that their AAD Connect suddenly stopped synchronizing for no apparent reason.Customer may have received Unhealthy Identity Synchronization Notification emails from MSOnlineServicesTeam@MicrosoftOnline.com The Synchronization Service Manager UI indicates that regular sync cycles stopped around July, 21st, 2017Checking Sync Scheduler status using in Get-ADSyncScheduler in PowerShell confirms that sync is suspended indicated by SchedulerSuspended being set to True and NextSyncCycleStartTimeInUTC, whichidentifies the next sync cycle, having the date in the past when AAD Connect stopped synchronizing.Additional signatures that confirm a scenario match:Looking up at the logs under %ProgramData%\AADConnect, we can confirm that AutoUpgrade was attempted on the same date that server stopped synchronizingThe latest SyncEngine-AutoUpgrader-date-time.log shows that version 1.1.561.0 was installed by AutoUpgrade. Azure AD Connect Upgrade Verbose: 903 : ========================================================================== Azure AD Connect Upgrade Verbose: 903 : Sync Engine Auto Upgrade Starting: 07/21/2017 13:30:52 Azure AD Connect Upgrade Verbose: 903 : ========================================================================== Azure AD Connect Upgrade Verbose: 903 : Upgrading AADConnect to version: 1.1.561.0Control Panel/Programs and Features or Sync UI Help | About shows AAD Connect version 1.1.561.0 is installedBut (Get-ADSyncGlobalSettings).Parameters | select Name,Value indicates that Server Configuration version under Global Settings is from an old buildNote: In this case there�s a mismatch between AAD Connect synchronization services version (1.1.561) and the server configuration version (1.1.281). Launching the Wizard, shows a pending Upgrade, meaning that latest AAD Connect setup wizard was not run yet. Application event logs around the date when server stopped synchronizing indicate an access violation exception error caused by AAD Connect Upgrader process: Log Name: Application Source: .NET Runtime Date: 7/21/2017 1:35:02 PM Event ID: 1026 Task Category: None Level: Error Keywords: Classic User: N/A Computer: O365SYN5.O365.NuAlex.net Description: Application: Microsoft.Azure.ActiveDirectory.Synchronization.Upgrader.exe Framework Version: v4.0.30319 Description: The process was terminated due to an unhandled exception. Exception Info: System.AccessViolationException Stack: at MmsServerRCW.IMMSServer2.GetSchedulerConnectorOverride(System.Guid ByRef, Int32 ByRef, Int32 ByRef) at MmsServerRCW.IMMSServer2.GetSchedulerConnectorOverride(System.Guid ByRef, Int32 ByRef, Int32 ByRef) at Microsoft.DirectoryServices.MetadirectoryServices.UI.WebServices.MMSWebService.GetSchedulerConnectorOverride(System.Guid) at Microsoft.IdentityManagement.PowerShell.Cmdlet.SetADSyncSchedulerConnectorOverrideCmdlet.ProcessRecord() at System.Management.Automation.CommandProcessor.ProcessRecord() at System.Management.Automation.CommandProcessorBase.DoExecute() at System.Management.Automation.Internal.PipelineProcessor.SynchronousExecuteEnumerate(System.Object, System.Collections.Hashtable, Boolean) at System.Management.Automation.Runspaces.LocalPipeline.InvokeHelper() at System.Management.Automation.Runspaces.LocalPipeline.InvokeThreadProc() at System.Threading.ExecutionContext.RunInternal(System.Threading.ExecutionContext, System.Threading.ContextCallback, System.Object, Boolean) at System.Threading.ExecutionContext.Run(System.Threading.ExecutionContext, System.Threading.ContextCallback, System.Object, Boolean) at System.Threading.ExecutionContext.Run(System.Threading.ExecutionContext, System.Threading.ContextCallback, System.Object) at System.Threading.ThreadHelper.ThreadStart()
rootCause: In July 2017, the AAD Connect team expanded the scope of AAD Connect sync clients eligible for AutoUpgrade. However, the AAD Connect Upgrader binaries that are downloaded by the AutoUpgrade process took a DLL dependency from AAD Connect version 1.1.484 that causes the Upgrader to crash when trying to upgrade older versions of AAD Connect where the updated DLL is not present. Since the AutoUpgrade scope has been increased to include more AAD Connect configurations, AutoUpgrade fails for older versions of AAD Connect installations which caught many older builds that Customers didn�t upgrade for many months. Customers that were already in version 1.1.484 or later were not affected by this issue.In summary, synchronization fails after 1.1.557 and later versions of AAD Connect attempt to auto-upgrade versions 1.1.443 and earlier of AAD Connect, for the following root cause:A new DCOM API change was introduced in build 1.1.484.Auto-Upgrade took a dependency on the DCOM API change in build 1.1.557 and later.As part of the process of auto-upgrading to build 1.1.557 and later, the Upgrader process attempts to query DCOM.If AAD Connect is being upgraded from more recent versions 1.1.484 and after, DCOM DLL already contains the Upgrader dependency and the upgrade process completes correctly without issue.However, in cases where the old DCOM DLL loaded in memory is from a build prior to 1.1.484, then the API is incompatible and AccessViolation exception occurs.
solution: AAD Connect team already removed the latest version from AutoUpgrade so there�s no more instances of this issue and we are planning/testing a new AAD Connect version that will be pushed by AutoUpgrade and fix the issue for all broken customers. In the meanwhile, many customers may call Support to address this issue so here�s the steps required to mitigate the problem.Launch AAD Connect Wizard and click Upgrade � should only ask for the AAD credentials.After completing the Upgrade, Exit the Wizard and close any PowerShell windows.Confirm again the Global Settings from PowerShell and it should show the Server Configuration version updated to 1.1.561.0: (Get-ADSyncGlobalSettings).Parameters | select Name,ValueOpen AAD Connect Wizard again and go to View current configuration IMPORTANT: Check if features like PasswordSync or Password Writeback are ok. If there�s any feature that shows as Disabled that was previously enabled, Customer needs to go ahead and enable these features again.

symptom: AbstractThere is an issue that is causing Automatic Upgrade to not work correctly for Azure AD Connect servers with version 1.1.443.0 and below. Azure AD Connect servers affected by this issue are left in one of the following states: The server is partially upgraded. The scheduler is suspended, and no automatic synchronization cycle occurs. The server is upgraded. The scheduler is enabled, and object changes are synchronized correctly to Azure AD. However, Password Sync feature and/or Password Writeback feature are disabled after upgrade. SymptomsTo determine whether your Azure AD Connect server is affected by this issue: 1. Determine whether Automatic Upgrade recently attempted to upgrade your server by looking at the log files under folder %ProgramData%\AADConnect. The presence of log files with title SyncEngine-AutoUpgrader-[Date]-[Time].log indicates that Automatic Upgrade occurred and when. 2. If Automatic Upgrade has occurred in the month of July 2017, verify whether Automatic Upgrade has left the server in partially upgraded state: o Start the Azure AD Connect wizard. If the server is partially upgraded, you will be prompted to upgrade your Azure AD Connect server. o Compare the binary version of the Azure AD Connect with its server configuration version. During automatic upgrade, the binaries are upgraded first, followed by the server configuration. If they are different, the server is partially-upgraded. To check the server binary version, go to Control Panel Add/Remove Program Files and look for Microsoft Azure AD Connect. To check the server configuration version, run cmdlet (Get-ADSyncGlobalSettings).Parameters | select Name,Value and look for Microsoft.Synchronize.ServerConfigurationVersion: o Check whether the Sync Scheduler is in suspended state by running cmdlet Get-ADSyncScheduler. If the SchedulerSuspended property is true, the scheduler is suspended. 3. If the server isnt in partially upgraded state, and you have previously enabled Password Synchronization feature and Password Writeback feature on the server, verify that the features are enabled by selecting *View current configuration* in the Azure AD Connect wizard:
rootCause: There is a problem in AAD Connect Upgrader that causes the process Microsoft.Azure.ActiveDirectory.Synchronization.Upgrader.exe to terminate due to an unhandled exception, leaving the auto-upgrade setup incomplete.
solution: If the server is left in partially-upgraded state by Automatic Upgrade:Perform a manual, in-place upgrade on the server by starting the Azure AD Connect wizard and clicking Upgrade. For details on how to perform in-place upgrade, refer to this article.Once the Upgrade is complete, repeat the steps described earlier to confirm that the server is no longer in partially upgraded state.If you have previously enabled Password Synchronization feature and/or Password Writeback feature, verify that the feature remains enabled after the Upgrade is complete.If the features have been disabled by Automatic Upgrade, you must manually enable them by selecting *Customize synchronization options* in the Azure AD Connect wizard:

symptom: AbstractThis article explains mitigation steps and provides some best practices to create a custom Sync Rule in Synchronization Rules Editor when the following error occurs:"Object reference not set to an instance of an object" SymptomsWhen creating a new Sync Rule in Synchronization Rules Editor UI, error "Object reference not set to an instance of an object" appears after clicking "Add" on the last Sync Rule configuration page.
rootCause: Normally, when adding a new custom Sync Rule by clicking in "Add new rule", it's not mandatory to provide a Tag string, but due to a bug in AAD Connect 1.1.561, the Synchronization Rules Editor will throw the error shown above if Tag textbox is empty. The Tag is present on the first Sync Rule configuration page as shown below.This issue is limited to the Synchronization Rules Editor UI as PowerShell cmdlet 'New-ADSyncRule' works correctly without specifying a Tag.The fix for this bug has been checked-in and will be included in the Upcoming AAD Connect August Release.
solution: There's two possible workarounds available for this issue:Create the new custom Sync Rule using Powershell New-ADSyncRule cmdletORType a custom Tag string on the first Sync Rule configuration page (Description).Best PracticeMicrosoft recommends you create this tag with a name that is unique to your company, identifies the purpose of the rule, and indicates a version. For example, a rule that performs custom filtering of users might be called: Contoso.CloudFilteredUser.001.WARNING: All rules starting with Microsoft.* are subject to deletion or revision during upgrades. To guarantee custom rules are preserved they must not start with a Microsoft prefix.

symptom: Abstract This article describes the symptoms when file "C:\ProgramData\AADConnect\PersistedState.xml" is missing or is corrupted. The PersistedState.xml file holds important data about current AAD Connect Synchronization settings and AD FS integration that is necessary to run AAD Connect Wizard. If this file is deleted or corrupted, the Wizard will completely lose out current server configuration state or may throw the exceptions errors described below. The purpose of this article is to show how to identity and recover a broken AAD Connect Wizard due to the PersistedState file being corrupted or missing by restoring a backup of the PersistedState file so that the Wizard can work again.SymptomsThere are 3 different symptoms whether the PersistedState file - "C:\ProgramData\AADConnect\PersistedState.xml" - is missing a) and b), or is corrupted c).a) Launching the Wizard when PersistedState file is missingThe wizard won�t find the file and therefore thinks AAD Connect is being set up for the first time. In this case, the user will have the same experience as they did when first launching it.b) An AAD Connect Upgrade when PersistedState file is missingAfter installing AzureADConnect.msi the Wizard shows the �Upgrade Azure Active Directory Connect� a page. If the Customer goes ahead and clicks the Upgrade button, setup will fail with the errors below: Trace log error: System.ArgumentNullException: Value cannot be null. Parameter name: state[01:08:36.767] [ 12] [INFO ] Calling to get the last dir sync time for the current user[01:08:36.997] [ 12] [ERROR] A terminating unhandled exception occurred.Exception Data (Raw): System.AggregateException: One or more errors occurred. ---> System.ArgumentNullException: Value cannot be null.Parameter name: state at Microsoft.Online.Deployment.Types.PersistedState.MicrosoftOnlinePersistedStateProvider.SetStateElementInternal(PersistedStateElement element, PersistedStateContainer state) at Microsoft.Online.Deployment.Types.PersistedState.MicrosoftOnlinePersistedStateProvider.SetStateElement(PersistedStateElement element) at Microsoft.Online.Deployment.OneADWizard.UI.WizardPages.AzureTenantPageViewModel.ValidateCredentials() at System.Threading.Tasks.Task.Execute() --- End of inner exception stack trace ------> (Inner Exception #0) System.ArgumentNullException: Value cannot be null.Parameter name: state at Microsoft.Online.Deployment.Types.PersistedState.MicrosoftOnlinePersistedStateProvider.SetStateElementInternal(PersistedStateElement element, PersistedStateContainer state) at Microsoft.Online.Deployment.Types.PersistedState.MicrosoftOnlinePersistedStateProvider.SetStateElement(PersistedStateElement element) at Microsoft.Online.Deployment.OneADWizard.UI.WizardPages.AzureTenantPageViewModel.ValidateCredentials() at System.Threading.Tasks.Task.Execute()<--- Wizard Upgrade error: ArgumentNullException � Value cannot be null. Parameter name: statec) When PersistedState file is corruptedWhether the user is launching the Wizard or upgrading AAD Connect, when the Wizard launches while the PersistedState file is corrupted, the user will immediately see an error: InvalidOperationException � There is an error in XML document (x, y).
rootCause: In the AAD Connect upgrade path, the PersistedState file is written over with a blank copy and subsequently filled with contents later on. However, if the application hits an unexpected error before the second write happens, the file will never be updated, and the server is left with a blank file. This behavior has been corrected in the August release of AAD Connect. There were also some instances where Customers deleted this file or folder by mistake.
solution: In order to fix this issue, Customer should restore the file/folder from the latest server backup. If customer does not have a backup of the disk or if the backup is not up-to-date, follow the steps below to use to restore from a previous backup file:a) Go to the folder where the file exists, i.e. C:\ProgramData\AADConnect\, there should be backup file(s) named �Backup-PersistedState-[timestamp].xml�. e.g.: Backup-PersistedState-20170825-010641.xmlb) Please move off the corrupted PersistedState.xml file so that we can analyze it to determine what happened (if possible).c) Locate the latest backup file and rename it to �PersistedState.xml�.d) Customer should be able to launch the Wizard to execute a task or proceed with the Upgrade. However, any state changes after the last backup file was created will be lost. NOTE: If you got a corrupted PersistedState.xml from a Customer please sent it over by email to Jody Hendrix <jodyh@microsoft.com> for root cause analysis, referring this Article ID 4040732.

symptom:
As of today, there are different portals that both Admins and
Users can access in a seamless way but which are developed by different teams
inside Microsoft and consequently have different implementations the user
photo. So, it�s important to understand that Microsoft online services are
composed by many different systems and workloads that may or may not be
connected with respect to the thumbnailPhoto and can show different behaviors
depending ok a) where is it reading the photo from; b) if there�s caching
involved and; c)  what�s the User license assigned.As mentioned before, there�s many portals involved in this
testing but I can only be authoritative for the Azure Portal and Account
portal. For any further questions or concerns about Office 365 portal you need
to escalate to the Office/Exchange Online team. Regarding the thumbnailPhoto attribute flow, the picture in
local AD has some limitations as it can only hold a low resolution image but
assuming the value in correctly stored in AD, AAD Connect then synchronizes the
photo into the thumbnailPhoto attribute in Azure AD. If the user does not have
an EXO license, the synchronization stops there. But when the user has an EXO
license assigned, then the thumbnailPhoto in Azure AD is copied to the
respective User�s Mailbox. This means that there�s 2 separate repositories of
the user photo in the cloud � the Azure AD thumbnailPhoto  and the
User Mailbox. These 2 repositories can get �out-of-sync� when the photo is
updated directly in the Office 365 Admin/User portal (or Set-UserPhoto) because
this process will store an HD picture in the user�s mailbox and from this point
on will
not accept any more updates coming
from the low resolution Azure AD thumbnailPhoto. In order to explain the different intricacies of the user photo
I used a simple lab with AAD Connect to test the synchronization of the
thumbnailPhoto from local AD and test the impact of updating the photo again
after it was synchronized for the first time. It didn�t require any special
tool or access rights, so this can also be tested by Support or Customer in
case there�s any doubts. These were the portals tested: 
 Account Portal (Azure AD only)
     - https://account.activedirectory.windowsazure.com/r#/applications
 Azure Portal (Cache delay) - https://portal.azure.com/#blade/Microsoft_AAD_IAM/UsersManagementMenuBlade/AllUsers
 O365 Admin Portal (Azure AD and
     User Mailbox) - https://admin.microsoft.com/Adminportal/Home
 O365 User Portal (Cache
     delay)- https://outlook.office365.com/owa/ , https://nam.delve.office.com/ , OneNote and other Office 365 workloads� Here�s a summary of the results for each portal: 
 Account Portal is the simplest
     implementation. It always reads the user photo from Azure AD
     thumbnailPhoto and doesn�t have any caching - This portal will always show
     the most updated image that is stored in Azure AD thumbnailPhoto, i.e.
     that is synchronized from on-premises AD with AAD Connect. 
 Azure Portal, on the other
     hand, is the most complex. Please follow notes below from the PG � This
     portal will show the picture from the User Mailbox or the Azure AD
     thumbnailPhoto for non-licensed users but there�s a caching delay. From my
     testing it can take up to 6 hours to display the updated photo.The images are
retrieved from multiple sources in the Microsoft_AAD_IAM extension in the azure
portal. Most blades retrieve them from a redis cache for performance reasons.
On those blades, depending upon how many times the cache is hit, the image may
not refresh for quite a while. This cache path also has had some issues with
certain images that causes them to be thrown out from the cache. That explains
why some images never show. We are aware of these issues, but are currently not
resourced to resolve it, because very few people notice, and it does not block
any primary scenarios.Some blades should show
the updated image immediately (the groups area) because they bypass the older
redis implementation and fetch the images directly from msgraph. This direct
fetch is the method we plan to use going forward, but there is no telling when
older blades will switch over to it. 
 The O365 Admin Portal will read
     the photo from the User Mailbox or Azure AD thumbnailPhoto if the user
     does not have a Mailbox (i.e. no EXO license assigned) � This portal will
     show the most updated photo (either from the mailbox or from the
     thumbnailPhoto) with no delays because there�s no caching involved. 
 The O365 User Portal(s) will
     always read the photo from the User Mailbox. If the user doesn�t have a
     Mailbox then it should show a blank picture since there�s no place to
     retrieve the photo, unless there�s a specific workload capable of reading
     the Azure AD thumbnailPhoto, similarly to O365 Admin Portal. � This
     portal(s) will show the picture from the User Mailbox with a delay since
     there�s also caching involved.  Conclusions With the exception of the Account Portal (which can only ready
Azure AD thumbnailPhoto) all these portals should show the same picture as long
as the user has a mailbox, but you might have to wait a few hours until you can
see the updated photo.If the Account Portal is not a concern, the best method to keep
user�s photo in sync is to discard AD thumbnailPhoto. Just make sure the user
has a Mailbox (i.e. EXO license assigned) and use the O365 Portal or
Set-UserPhoto to upload the HD image into the mailbox. In this case, allow some
time (+6hours) to see the updated picture in Azure Portal due to the cache
delay as well as other Office 365 portals.To guarantee the user photo is the same on all portals,
including Account Portal, then when a user gets its HD picture updated through
Office Portal or Set-UserPhoto, you need to also update your local AD
thumbnailPhoto with the same picture and let Azure AD Connect synchronize it to
Azure AD thumbnailPhoto.Troubleshooting Tips 
 Have the user go to Account
     Portal. This is a good check if you just want to confirm if the photo was
     correctly updated from on-premises AD to Azure AD thumbnailPhoto with AAD
     Connect.
 Use AD Photo Edit tool in case
     you�re having issues synchronizing to Azure AD thumbnailPhoto. Using the
     AD Photo Edit feature "Resize
     to recommended dimensions� guarantees that the picture is not
     exceeding AD thumbnailPhoto limitations
 After updating Azure AD
     thumbnailPhoto you can immediately check in Office 365 Admin. If the
     updated picture is shown, then it was copied to the User�s mailbox, if not
     then the User Mailbox already contains an HD picture and is not accepting
     low resolution images from Azure AD thumbnailPhoto.
 After updating Azure AD
     thumbnailPhoto, wait at least 6 hours to see the updated photo shown in
     Office 365 User Portal or in Azure Portal
 After using Set-UserPhoto or
     Office 365 User Portal to update the HD photo in the User�s mailbox, wait
     at least 6 hours to see the updated photo shown in Office 365 User Portal
     or in Azure Portal

Abstract

This article explains a common issue that is by design when
User Writeback is still enabled

The User Writeback is a feature that allows customers to
create and manage users in the cloud, similarly to cloud-only accounts, that
are then synchronized back to on-premises AD. This feature however never
reached GA and some customers enabled this feature while still in Preview.
Since this feature as been disabled on the client side � i.e. AAD Connect Sync
client � its very unlikely to still find customers with this feature enabled.

However, although User Writeback preview feature was disabled
and removed from the Wizard back in Azure AD Connect August 2015 release, this
feature persists as enabled in the Azure AD tenant DirSyncFeatures property for
customers that enabled it during Preview. If you find a customer that still has
User Writeback enabled � either on the client side or tenant side - then you
should ask to disable this feature as explained in this article.

NOTE: please make sure you advise the Customer to not enable
this feature in any circumstances since it�s not supported and it can cause AD
data loss.

 

Symptoms

Customer I trying to softmatch
a user based on UPN or Proxy Address but ADSync engine always throws the error:

Unable to update this object because the
following attributes associated with this object have values that may already
be associated with another object in your local directory services:
[ProxyAddresses SMTP:user@contoso.com;UserPrincipalName user@contoso.com;].  Correct or remove the duplicate values in
your local directory.  Please refer to
http://support.microsoft.com/kb/2647098 for more information on identifying
objects with duplicate attribute values.

 

Even if you try to hardmatch
the user by stamping the same ImmutableID in the cloud-only account, you still
get the same error.
rootCause:
This happens when UserWriteback feature is still enabled on
the customer�s Azure AD tenant. When this feature is present,
DirSyncWebServices will not process any soft-match/hard-match logic. Since users
are not �matched� together by DirSyncWebServices logic, the sync engine shows the
uniqueness exception error above. This is By
Design.

A customer can check if the tenant has this feature enabled on
the tenant by running the following command:

 

Get-MsolDirSyncFeatures | select DirSyncFeature,Enabled Support can also check this feature in ViewPoint details or
by looking in DirSyncFeatures tenant property using MSODS Explorer (GME):

 

DirSyncFeatures (1):
EnableSoftMatchOnUpn, UserWriteback,
SynchronizeUpnForManagedUsers [10272]
solution:
Firstly, we need to disable UserWriteback feature using AAD
Connect Wizard and then we can
go ahead and convert any existent �UserWriteback-users� in the cloud to normal cloud-only users from the back-end. New versions of AAD Connect will
automatically disable UserWriteback feature on the client side. 

WARNING: If this feature is no longer enabled on the client
then it�s safe to disable UserWriteback on the tenant too, but if it�s still
enabled we strongly recommend to backup any AD data associated with any
existent �UserWriteback-users� because this process may delete these objects
from AD!

 You can check if there�s any existent �UserWriteback-users�
in the cloud by running a query in MSODS Explorer (GME), as follow:Open the tenant in MSODS ExplorerGo to Objects
tabRun the query: (any) | HasOnPremisesShadow | == |
True







4.      
Any objects returned with HasOnPremisesShadow=True
are �UserWriteback-users� that may or may not have a corresponding user in AD.
This may help the customer identify which AD users were created using this
feature.

 Here�s what you need to do in order to check if User Writeback is enabled on the
client side:Run
     C:\Program Files\Microsoft Azure Active Directory
     Connect>AzureADConnect.exe /ShowUserWritebackGo
     through the Wizard until you reach the page for Optional FeaturesAt
     this page you should be able to see if User Writeback is enabled or
     disabled and then close the wizardIf this feature is still enabled on AAD Connect Wizard,
please update to the latest AAD Connect version which will safely disable the feature.
WARNING: Unchecking the User writeback option and finishing the
configuration on the Wizard will delete any �UserWriteback-users� from AD! If there�s
any �UserWriteback-users� still present in AD and User Writeback is still enabled
after AAD Connect upgrade, please escalate you support case.After confirming that User Writeback is already disabled on
AAD Connect Wizard then we can go ahead and disable it on the cloud, which will
also convert any AAD users with HasOnPremisesShadow=True and fix the soft-match
issue. 







Please raise an escalation to 'Customer Escalations - AAD Connect' to have User Writeback feature disabled from the back-end and fix-up any �UserWriteback-users�

for this offering in the Phoenix catalog is hereThe link to the
repository on corp is here. Do not under any circumstances publish related scripts outside of Microsoft. Expands on the script written by Ming Chen referenced in internal KB 4015604 EXOADFSAL: PowerShell script parses ADFS security event logs to identify caller IPs + targeted UPN names That version of Get-ADFSLockoutStat.ps1 is available for download from scriptcenter @ https://gallery.technet.microsoft.com/scriptcenter/Event-411-reader-Export-a6cd78daEnhancements in the  ADFS Health Monitoring and Analytics Premium version of Get-ADFSLockoutStat.ps1  includeAccepts the name of the
     computers to collect and analyze.
Specifically, you can specify the names of the ADFS servers to collect 411 event logs from without the need to save the .evtx files to a directory to then parse the events of interest. The 
     "Collect" and/or "Analyze" have been separated to allow collecting data
     from one machine and later analyzing them on another. Example syntax: .\Get-ADFSLockoutStat.ps1 -computername memberserver1,serverserver2 -collect -analyzeIt collects the following information:The account lockout     policy of the domain that the servers belong to. The audit policy and     ADFS auditing necessary to get events 411 on the serversThe security event log     of the servers. GET_ADFSLockoutStatus.XLSX contains the following workbooks:The list of events ID 411 collected on the servers.The list of failed logon attempts per account name and source IP address.The list of failed logon attempts per source IP address and account name.The list of failed logon attempts per source IP address, account name and time.The list of failed logon attempts per account name, source IP address and time.Sample Output Get-ADFSLockoutStat.log Contains advanced parameters with the following arguments: MaxExport: Maximum number of events to export per each evtx. 5000 events are exported by default.MaxImport: Maximum number of events to import into excel. 5000 events are imported by default.StartTime: Earliest 411 events to export.MaxThreads: Maximum number of concurrent jobs that can be run in parallel. AbstractThe
following Azure AD Connect versions have 2 issues with the Change user sign-in task:1.1.557.01.1.558.01.1.561.01.1.614.0Both issues impact Pass-through Authentication customers who
don�t want to use Password Hash
Synchronization. Both issues were fixed in Azure
AD Connect version 1.1.644.0.Overview

The issue occurs in the following two scenarios:Issue 1: Password Synchronization is enabled when User sign-in method is
set to Pass-through Authentication

Scenario
1: You have an existing Azure AD Connect deployment where both Password Synchronization and
Pass-through Authentication are disabled.
After which, you enable Pass-through Authentication using the Change user sign-in task:In the Azure AD Connect wizard, you choose the Change user sign-in task.On the User sign-in page, you choose Pass-through Authentication option.On completion of the wizard, Password Hash Synchronization is
automatically enabled.Scenario
2: You have an existing Azure AD Connect deployment where Password
Synchronization is disabled, and
Pass-through Authentication is enabled.
After which, you toggle the Seamless
Single Sign-on using the Change user
sign-in task:In the Azure AD Connect wizard, you choose the Change user sign-in task.On the User sign-in page, the Pass-through Authentication option is
already configured.You check/uncheck the Enable single sign-on option.On completion of the wizard, Password Hash Synchronization is automatically
enabled.

















The background on this issue is as follows:

Prior to Azure AD Connect version 1.1.557.0,
Password Synchronization was a pre-requisite for enabling Pass-through
Authentication. Therefore, Password Synchronization was enabled when
Pass-through Authentication was enabled.Subsequently, Password Synchronization was no
longer required for Pass-through Authentication. As part of Azure AD Connect
version 1.1.557.0, a change was introduced to Azure AD Connect to remove the
enablement of Password Synchronization when Pass-through Authentication is
enabled during Azure AD Connect installation.However, the same change was not applied to the Change user sign-in task. If you use
the task to enable Pass-through Authentication to an existing Azure AD Connect deployment,
Password Synchronization is automatically enabled. This issue is fixed in Azure
AD Connect version 1.1.644.0 to ensure that Password Hash Synchronization
remains disabled when Pass-through Authentication is enabled using the Change user sign-in task.





Issue 2: Incorrect prompt about disablement of Password Synchronization
when User sign-in method is set to Pass-through Authentication

Scenario:
You have an existing Azure AD Connect deployment where Password Synchronization
is enabled, and Pass-through
Authentication is disabled. After
which, you enable Pass-through
Authentication using the Change user
sign-in task:In the Azure AD Connect wizard, you chose the Change user sign-in task.On the User sign-in page, you chose Pass-through Authentication option.Before applying the change, the wizard
incorrectly displays a prompt that that Password Hash Synchronization will be disabled.On completion of the wizard, Password Hash Synchronization remains
enabled.









The background on this issue is as follows:

By design, if Password Hash Synchronization is
enabled, changing the user sign-in task to any other option does not disable
Password Hash Synchronization. The issue is fixed in Azure
AD Connect version 1.1.644.0 to ensure that the prompt about the
disablement of Password Hash Synchronization is not displayed.
solution:
Follow these steps to mitigate this issue on your tenant:Run Azure AD Connect and click View current configuration. Check if Password synchronization is enabled on your tenant.Disable the Password
synchronization feature:Run Azure AD Connect and click Configure.Choose the Customize
synchronization options task.On the Optional
features page, uncheck the Password
synchronization feature.Complete the wizard.Optionally, if
you want to clear password hashes already synchronized to Azure AD, follow
these steps:Ensure that the Password writeback feature is disabled
on your tenant:Run Azure AD Connect and click Configure.Choose the Customize
synchronization options task.On the Optional
features page, uncheck the Password writeback
feature.Complete the wizard.Use the Set-MsolUserPassword cmdlet to set
random passwords on all impacted users. You need to run this cmdlet 5 times for
each user because Azure AD stores the password hash history on each user (last
4 password hashes).Feedback or questions?

If you have any feedback or questions, email us at aadopauthfeedback@microsoft.com.

Abstract

This article describes an issue where device objects that
are synchronized into the on-premises AD DS from Azure AD, using Device Writeback
in AAD Connect, may get deleted due to inactivity.  

 

Symptoms

Conditional access policies to applications and services
that require device authentication fail.
rootCause:
If device authentication is required, the user must have
registered their device and the device object synchronized into the local AD
DS.  The device is associated with the
device object and ADFS verifies this when evaluating the access policies.  If the device object doesn�t exist, the
evaluation will fail.

 

The device registration service will periodically check all
device objects in the directory and clean them up due it inactivity.  This is done to reduce AD DS bloat.  However, since the object is mastered in AAD,
the objects won�t get updated and DRS will end up deleting valid objects.
solution:
The workaround to this problem is to disable device clean-up
in ADFS. In AD FS 2016 this is set on the properties
dialog for the Device Registration Service in the ADFS admin MMC snap-in as shown below.Customers in ADFS 2012/R2 need to use PowerShell to disable this
device clean-up using the following command:  



Set-AdfsDeviceRegistration -MaximumInactiveDays 0 Note: Setting MaximumInactiveDays to value 0 disables the cleanup task.
